description: A complete beginner Python course. This file is the BASELINE CURRICULUM: the AI does not invent the course, it transforms, personalizes and gamifies the content below. Replace the placeholder content in each module with your real course material — the structure (concept, learning_objectives, explanation, examples, common_mistakes, exercise_patterns) is what the generator consumes.

modules.title: Variables and Assignment
learning_objectives: Understand that a variable is a named container for a value
Assign values to variables with the = operator
Read the value of a variable after assignment
Reassign a variable to a new value
modules.explanation: A variable is a name that refers to a value stored in memory. You create a variable with an assignment statement: name = value. The name goes on the left, the value on the right. Once assigned, you can use the name anywhere you would use the value. Assigning again replaces the old value. Variable names should be lowercase_with_underscores and describe what they hold.
examples: score = score + 10  # reassignment using the old value

lives = 3
lives = lives - 1  # lives is now 2
common_mistakes: Writing the value on the left: 10 = score (SyntaxError)
Using a variable before assigning it (NameError)
exercise_patterns: Assign a given value to a named variable
Update a variable based on its current value

modules.title: Basic Types: int, float, str, bool
learning_objectives: Distinguish integers, floats, strings and booleans
Convert between types with int(), float(), str()
Use type() to inspect a value
modules.explanation: Every value in Python has a type. Whole numbers are int, decimal numbers are float, text wrapped in quotes is str, and True/False are bool. Types determine what operations make sense: you can add two ints, but adding an int to a str raises a TypeError. Convert explicitly with int("5"), float(3), or str(42).
common_mistakes: Adding a number to a string without converting
Quoting numbers that should be numeric ("5" + 1 fails)
exercise_patterns: Convert user-style string input to a number and compute with it

modules.title: Working with Strings
learning_objectives: Create and concatenate strings
Use f-strings to build messages from variables
Use common string methods: upper, lower, strip, replace
Index and slice strings
modules.explanation: Strings are sequences of characters. Combine them with + or, better, build them with f-strings: f"Hello {name}". Strings have methods: "abc".upper() gives "ABC". Index characters with s[0] and slice with s[1:4]. Strings are immutable: methods return new strings.
common_mistakes: Forgetting the f prefix on an f-string
Expecting s.upper() to modify s in place
exercise_patterns: Build a formatted message from given variables

modules.title: Making Decisions with if/elif/else
learning_objectives: Write if statements with comparison operators
Add elif and else branches
Combine conditions with and / or / not
Understand indentation defines the block
modules.explanation: Conditionals let a program choose between paths. An if statement evaluates a condition; if it is True, the indented block below it runs. elif checks another condition when the previous ones were False, and else runs when nothing matched. Comparisons use ==, !=, <, >, <=, >=. Combine conditions with and/or, negate with not. Indentation (4 spaces) is how Python knows which lines belong to the branch.
common_mistakes: Using = instead of == in a condition
Forgetting the colon at the end of the if line
Wrong indentation so a line is outside the branch
exercise_patterns: Set a variable to different values depending on a threshold
Fix a condition that uses = instead of ==

modules.title: Repeating Work with Loops
learning_objectives: Use for loops with range() to repeat a fixed number of times
Loop over the items of a list or string
Accumulate a result across iterations
Use while loops for open-ended repetition
modules.explanation: Loops automate repetition. A for loop runs its block once per item: for i in range(5) runs 5 times with i = 0..4; for item in items visits each element of a collection. A common pattern is the accumulator: start total = 0 before the loop and add to it inside. A while loop repeats as long as its condition stays True — make sure something inside changes the condition or the loop never ends.
common_mistakes: Resetting the accumulator inside the loop
while loops that never update their condition
exercise_patterns: Sum or count items with an accumulator
Repeat an action exactly N times

modules.title: Lists
learning_objectives: Create lists and access items by index
Append, remove and update items
Use len() and the in operator
Loop over lists
modules.explanation: PLACEHOLDER — replace with your course content. A list is an ordered, mutable collection: inventory = ["map", "rope"]. Access with inventory[0], add with .append(), remove with .remove(), measure with len().
exercise_patterns: Manage a collection: add, remove, check membership

modules.title: Dictionaries
learning_objectives: Create dicts of key-value pairs
Read, add and update entries
Use .get() and the in operator
Loop over keys and values
modules.explanation: PLACEHOLDER — replace with your course content. A dictionary maps keys to values: stats = {"hp": 100, "mp": 30}. Read with stats["hp"], write with stats["hp"] = 90, read safely with stats.get("xp", 0).

modules.title: Functions
learning_objectives: Define functions with def
Use parameters and return values
Call functions and use their results
modules.explanation: PLACEHOLDER — replace with your course content. Functions package reusable behavior: def heal(amount): return hp + amount. Parameters receive inputs, return sends a result back to the caller.
exercise_patterns: Write a function matching a described behavior and signature

modules.title: Basic Debugging
learning_objectives: Read tracebacks to locate errors
Recognize SyntaxError, NameError, TypeError, IndexError
Use print() to inspect program state
modules.explanation: PLACEHOLDER — replace with your course content. A traceback points to the line where Python gave up. Read the last line first (the error type and message), then the line number.
exercise_patterns: Fix a short broken program so its tests pass

modules.title: Mini Final Project
learning_objectives: Combine variables, conditionals, loops, lists and functions in one small program
modules.explanation: PLACEHOLDER — replace with your course content. The final project combines every previous concept into one capstone exercise, framed by the story's final challenge.
exercise_patterns: Multi-step program with several tested behaviors
